CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

{"body":"![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/abdd22e7-10e7-4047-b102-a4d13523798b)

Image From [Mitochondrial protein transport: Versatility of translocases and mechanisms - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36931257/) 

We have modelled each import pathway based on the start and destination, plus the maturation steps. We have modelled D. melanogaster and fission yeast, and the models are equivalent with very minor differences (eg. MIM complex in yeast is replaced by a functionally analogous system in metazoa).

This is the set of terms required to describe all mitochondrial import pathways:
	
- [x] 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport)
Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria.

- [x] 2. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion~ (1. In the image)
alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
Narrow synonym MIM pathway (different complex in higher eukaryotes)
Narrow synonym: MTCH pathway
Definition: The import of alpha helical and tail-anchored proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane.
Note:In yeast, the insertase is the mitochondrial import (MIM) complex, composed of Mim1 and Mim2. In higher eukaryotes, the functional counterpart MTCH performs a similar role despite lacking sequence and structural homology, reflecting convergent evolution (PMID:40782022)
 	Model: [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003239)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003187)]

- [x]  3.  [GO:0030150](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0030150)    protein import into mitochondrial matrix (2 in the image)
Synonym exact: TOM-TIM23-PAM presequence matrix import pathway
NewDefinition: The import of proteins with mitochondrial targeting sequence from the cytosol across the mitochondrial outer and inner membranes into the matrix via TOM-TIM23-PAM. 
Model:[[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800002828)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800007453)]
move to new structure

- [x] 4. GO_NEW ~TOM-TIM23-SORT protein import and lateral insertion into the inner membrane~ (2b in the  image)
TOM-TIM23-SORT-mediated protein insertion into the mitochondrial inner membrane
Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol across the outer mitochondrial membrane and laterally inserted into the inner membrane. Typically acting on single-pass transmembrane proteins with an N-terminal targeting sequence and an internal hydrophobic stop-transfer sequence which targets them for lateral insertion.
Note: Some proteins utilize this pathway but can be further processed and released from the inner membrane into the IMS. 
 	Model: [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00003678)] [[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00005852)]

- [x] 5. KEEP [GO:0045041](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0045041)  protein import into mitochondrial intermembrane space
New Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol across the outer mitochondrial membrane into the intermembrane space.
          - [ ] rename child [GO:0160203](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0160203) protein import into the intermembrane space via the disulfide relay system (3/7 in the image)
New Definition: The import of small cysteine-containing proteins from the cytosol across the outer mitochondrial membrane via the TOM complex driven by oxidative folding.
Narrow synonym:  CHCHD4/Mia40-dependent import
Note: We retained the parent because it is currently not clear that all proteins imported by this pathway require the disulfide relay system.
 	Model:[[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00005509)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00004096)]
	
- [x] 6. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial beta barrel outer membrane protein insertion~ (5 in figure)
beta barrel protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
Synonym: TOM-SAM pathway
Definition: The import of beta barrel proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane.
 	Model  [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800002581)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69729a3800002651)]

- [ ] 7. GO_NEW ~TOM-TIM22 inner membrane protein import~  (6 in figure)
TOM-TIM22-mediated mitochondrial inner membrane protein insertion
Definition: The import of multipass-membrane proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial inner membrane, supported by tiny-tim chaperones.
Narrow synonym: carrier import pathway
Narrow synonym: carrier pathway
 	Model [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00000205)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6994852c00000143)]


Consider Obsoletion

- [ ] GO:0070585   protein localization to mitochondrion (80 EXP)
- [ ] GO:0072656   maintenance of protein location in mitochondrion (5 EXP)
- [ ] GO:0051204   protein insertion into mitochondrial membrane (unnecessary grouping term) (5 EXP)
- [ ] GO:0045040    protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane =>
Suggest GO new  x or y

- [ ] GO:1903637    negative regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
- [ ] GO:1903638    positive regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
- [ ] GO:1903636    regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
- [ ] GO:0045039	protein insertion into mitochondrial inner membrane

The 59  EXP (27 HTP)  to these regulation terms are mainly mitophagy or chaperones. All can be annotated to other more appropriate  terms
- [ ] GO:1903748    negative regulation of protein localization to mitochondrion
- [ ] GO:1903747    regulation of protein localization to mitochondrion

- [ ] GO:0070096 mitochondrial outer membrane translocase complex assembly 
Assembly of TOM isn’t a separate assembly process,  assembly is only affected by import pathways so the correct way to annotate this would be to add TOM subunits as targets of the appropriate import pathways.

":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4053709178","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xp9ly","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" A very brief summary of the yeast and human differences in the Ccs-dependent pathways: In yeast, Ccs1 is first imported via the Mia40/Erv1 disulfide relay system. Resident Ccs1 then recruits incoming Sod1 precursors by oxidative folding. A minor parallel route allows some Sod1 to enter the IMS independently of Ccs1, via Mia40 directly. In humans: CCS1 import is autocatalytic, with mature IMS-resident CCS1 acting as its own import receptor via oxidative folding. This reaction is ROS-sensitive -> mitochondrial oxidative stress directly increases IMS recruitment of both CCS1 and SOD1. Metalation and oxidative folding of Sod1: Sod1 requires copper and a zinc ion cofactors. Ccs1 directly mediates copper delivery to Sod1 and forms a transient intermolecular disulfide with Sod1 to facilitate copper transfer and disulfide bond formation simultaneously. Zinc incorporation is thought to occur independently of Ccs1, prior to the Ccs1 interaction. (this is generalised as still some debated details between human and yeast) ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T11:03:33Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4054309234","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xqQtm","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Could we model is as a single IMS branching pathway post TOM (this would be analogous to the amino acid biosynthesis pathways, we now only represent as a single pathway to get the same result. The mitochondrial version of superoxide dismutase is in the matrix not the IMS, and it isn't copper dependent (the cytosolic one is)...the mitochondrial one uses manganese as its cofactor ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:55:48Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055236224","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xtjnz","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"actually claude says Sod1 (Cu/Zn-SOD) — the cytoplasmic form Primarily found in the cytoplasm, though a small fraction can also be present in the mitochondrial intermembrane space but I don't know if this is true for fission yeast where the cytosolic form hasn't been detected in the mitochondria","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:57:28Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055251443","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xuaHA","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" my claude.ai says: In the mitochondrial IMS specifically, the same chemistry applies. CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.
